glossary.definition: A field of study involving the development of intelligent computer systems to support humans in completing various functions, activities, and coping with different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.

glossary.definition: A general concept possessed by humans, animals, and plants. For humans, it is studied in fields like philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, and psychology. It is debated whether it is a single ability or a set of abilities. A general definition should encompass the essence of different types, not be limited to specific senses, environments, goals, or structures, be based on fundamental principles, and ideally be formally expressed, objective, and practically realisable. Currently, a unified general definition does not exist due to limited knowledge of the world.
glossary.sentence: Nowadays, it is believed that humans, animals and even plants possess intelligence.

glossary.definition: Studied in-depth in fields such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, and psychology. Psychology has offered theories about its nature, defined characteristics, and developed tests for assessment. There is a crisis in defining it due to lack of unified viewpoint on whether it is a single or set of abilities, measurement problems due to diversity (linguistic, cultural, physical), and static nature of many tests. It serves as a standard for some definitions of artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc.

glossary.definition: A scientific field contributing to artificial intelligence by reinforcing the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines.
glossary.sentence: Psychology - Psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines.

glossary.definition: Knowledge about it is limited. It is mostly related to the learning ability of animals such as monkeys, dolphins, elephants, rats, or crows. It is more challenging to measure than human intelligence due to greater diversity in cognitive/perceptual capacities, sensory organs, and environmental exploration ways. Direct explanation of goals is not possible. Studies may be biased by expecting it to be based on the same principles as human intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Much less we know about animal intelligence.

glossary.definition: Knowledge about it is even more limited than animal intelligence. It is not based on a brain and nervous system. Plants possess intellectual performance forms such as learning based on repeated practice, memory, simultaneous perception of aspects (shape, colour, smell, sound), a “sense of place”, and awareness of the neighbourhood. Plants gather and update environmental information, integrate it with internal state, and make decisions reconciling well-being with the environment.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge about plant intelligence is even more limited.

glossary.definition: Systems developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or to cope with different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.

glossary.definition: A researcher who organized a summer school in 1956 with scientists interested in automata theory, neural networks, studies of intelligence, and language simulation. He offered the first definition of artificial intelligence, emphasizing its similarity to human intelligence.
glossary.sentence: The first definition of artificial intelligence was offered by John McCarthy in 1956.

glossary.definition: Researchers who grouped definitions of artificial intelligence into four categories in 2009. These categories focus on different aspects of intelligent performance (thought processes/reasoning vs. behaviour) and use two standards (human intelligence vs. rationality).
glossary.sentence: Meanwhile, Stuart Russell and Peter Norvig (2009) have grouped definitions of artificial intelligence into four categories.

glossary.definition: An approach underlying systems that think humanly. It considers the human being to be an information processing system. Research focuses on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly.

glossary.definition: Intelligent computer systems based on a cognitive modelling approach, which considers humans as information processing systems and focuses on information representation, processing, transformation, and storage in the mind and computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly.

glossary.definition: Intelligent computer systems based on logics. Knowledge is represented as logical rules to infer new knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Systems that think rationally are based on logics.

glossary.definition: A basis for systems that think rationally. Knowledge is represented as logical rules to infer new knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Systems that think rationally are based on logics.

glossary.definition: An approach underlying systems that act humanly.
glossary.sentence: The approach of the Turing test underlies systems that act humanly.

glossary.definition: Intelligent computer systems underlying the approach of the Turing test.
glossary.sentence: The approach of the Turing test underlies systems that act humanly.

glossary.definition: An ideal intelligence adopted as a standard in some definitions of artificial intelligence, specifically in categories on the right in Russell and Norvig's classification.
glossary.sentence: the categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard

glossary.definition: An event organized by John McCarthy with participation of scientists from the United States interested in automata theory, neural networks, studies of intelligence, and language simulation. The term 'artificial intelligence' was chosen for this school.
glossary.sentence: At that time, this researcher organized a summer school with the participation of scientists from the United States who were interested in automata theory, neural networks, studies of intelligence and language simulation.

glossary.definition: Agents that act to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: Any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it.
glossary.sentence: In this context, an agent is any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it.

glossary.definition: A fundamental test in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence, underlying systems that act humanly. It involves a tester asking questions to both an artificial intelligence and a human being using a text-based input tool without seeing them; if the tester cannot differentiate the answers, the artificial intelligence is considered intelligent.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence.

glossary.definition: A capability required for a computer system to pass the Turing test, enabling it to maintain successful communication with the tester as a human being does.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities... natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does

glossary.definition: A capability required for a computer system to pass the Turing test, used to store information acquired before and during the test.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities... knowledge representation, in order to store information acquired before and during the test.

glossary.definition: A capability required for a computer system to pass the Turing test, used to use stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities... automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions

glossary.definition: A capability required for a computer system to pass the Turing test, allowing it to adapt to a new situation, as well as to acquire and generalize patterns of different situations.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities... machine learning, in order to be able to adapt to a new situation, as well as to acquire and generalize patterns of different situations

glossary.definition: A variation of the Turing test that brings forward two more requirements: the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A capability required by the Total Turing test, allowing a computer to perceive objects.
glossary.sentence: ...the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects...

glossary.definition: A capability required by the Total Turing test, allowing a computer to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: ...and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A scientific field contributing to artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge encoded using some internal language.
glossary.sentence: Philosophy - Philosophers made possible artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge.

glossary.definition: A scientific field contributing to artificial intelligence by providing mathematical formalization in computing, logics, and probability theory, tools to work with logical statements and uncertain judgments, and foundations for reasoning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Mathematics - The transition from the philosophy to the formal science required mathematical formalization in three main areas: computing, logics, and probability theory.

glossary.definition: A scientific field contributing to artificial intelligence by offering theories on how to make choices that lead to desired results and laying grounds for studies of rational agents.
glossary.sentence: Economics - It offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.

glossary.definition: A scientific field contributing to artificial intelligence through research on how the human brain processes information, leading to the emergence of artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Neuroscience – Contributions of neuroscience in artificial intelligence is related to research on how the human brain processes information.

glossary.definition: Biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain, experiencing a heyday nowadays and closely related to deep learning. They find application in various fields such as text classification, student performance modelling, character recognition, credit card attrition, video surveillance, stock market prediction, cancer cell analysis, and vehicle scheduling. They can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks, and mainly for classification tasks in supervised machine learning.
glossary.sentence: Supervised Machine Learning

glossary.definition: A scientific field contributing to artificial intelligence by providing artefacts, computer speed, and memory capacity necessary for complex and large AI applications.
glossary.sentence: Computer engineering – Computer engineering provided artefacts, which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications.

glossary.definition: A scientific field contributing to artificial intelligence through feedback theory, offering the idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of a feedback mechanism.
glossary.sentence: Control science and cybernetics - The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory...

glossary.definition: A scientific field contributing to artificial intelligence by showing that language use fits the model of humans and animals as data processing machines, and contributing to natural language processing and knowledge representation.
glossary.sentence: Linguistics - Linguists showed that the use of language fits the model of psychologists that humans and animals can be considered as data processing machines.

glossary.definition: A goal of artificial intelligence related to understanding the principles that make possible intelligent performance in natural and artificial systems, involving analysis, hypothesis testing, and designing computational systems.
glossary.sentence: Goals of artificial intelligence can be considered along two dimensions... scientific goal is related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems.

glossary.definition: A goal of artificial intelligence related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.
glossary.sentence: Goals of artificial intelligence can be considered along two dimensions... engineering goal is related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems. Symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable. Symbolic artificial intelligence have a very good sense of reasoning for the solved problem.
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons). Connectionist representation of knowledge proves to be learnable. Connectionist artificial intelligence solves the problem but is not able to effectively reason the solution.
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons)

glossary.definition: A hypothesis defined by Newell and Simon in 1976 that defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved, formulated as: 'A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action'.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence is based on the physical symbol system hypothesis , defined by Newell and Simon in 1976. It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976)

glossary.definition: A system that includes symbols corresponding to meaningful physical patterns representing knowledge, experience, concepts, and causality; symbolic structures consisting of connected symbols depicting problem domain aspects; and operations for constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying structures. A physical system is capable of intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols.
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) . A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way.

glossary.definition: Meaningful physical patterns that could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts, and causality.
glossary.sentence: symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality

glossary.definition: Structures consisting of symbols connected to each other in some way, depicting important aspects of a problem domain.
glossary.sentence: symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain

glossary.definition: Actions such as constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying that allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures.
glossary.sentence: operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: Knowledge consisting mainly of generalisations available in the relevant problem domain, explicitly represented through a set of symbols in intelligent computer systems.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems based on this paradigm therefore explicitly represent problem domain knowledge (objects and their relations) through a set of symbols (Luger, 2009 ). This knowledge consists mainly of generalisations that are available in the relevant problem domain.

glossary.definition: A criticism described by John Searle where a person in a room manipulates Chinese symbols using an instruction manual to produce correct outputs without understanding the symbols, demonstrating that symbol manipulation does not equate to intelligence.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.

glossary.definition: Connected processing units in artificial neural networks that mimic the neurons of the human brain.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain

glossary.definition: Values attached to the connections between processing units in artificial neural networks, reflecting the strength of these connections, where knowledge is encoded and changed during network operation.
glossary.sentence: numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections. As a result, in connectionist artificial intelligence, knowledge is encoded as numerical values in the weights of the connections, which are changed during the operation of the network.

glossary.definition: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance; a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A program designed to play the board game Go, which complements artificial neural networks with tree-based search.
glossary.sentence: for example, tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go (Goel, 2021).

glossary.definition: Also called immature or limited artificial intelligence. Based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. This is the only approach in which significant progress has been achieved so far.
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.

glossary.definition: Also known as strong artificial intelligence or human-level intelligence. An approach that treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated. Related to the development of computer systems capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions. Attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human.
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) .

glossary.definition: An intelligence that surpasses human capabilities and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills.
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: Focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales. Asserts that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment. Based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world, since the world is the best model of itself; it is necessary to equip the system with sensors in order for the system to perceive the environment and actuators in order for the system to be able to operate in the environment. The main idea is that intelligence is a capability that arises when agents with simpler behaviours interact in the real environment and together achieve more complex behaviours.
glossary.sentence: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales” ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008).

glossary.definition: A paradigm under new artificial intelligence. Considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.

glossary.definition: Studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food. Used in search and optimisation.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: Studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish. Used in search and optimisation.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods that do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, and automatic reasoning.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: The environment containing different objects, their properties, relations with other objects, and functions, which needs to be transitioned to the world of intelligent computer systems.
glossary.sentence: Figure 1 shows that we have different objects, their properties, relations with other objects and functions in the real world.

glossary.definition: A way of structural organization of data about the real world.
glossary.sentence: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world

glossary.definition: Places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others.
glossary.sentence: data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others

glossary.definition: Ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.
glossary.sentence: algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: Also called a formal specification; uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: We need a formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification (Figure 3). The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: Also called a formal representation; uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: We need a formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: A process that can be seen as playing a game, represented in an intelligent computer system by using a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Playing a game can be seen as a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: The starting point of the problem-solving process; in the game of tic-tac-toe, this is a blank grid.
glossary.sentence: In this game, the initial state of the problem-solving process is a blank grid...

glossary.definition: The state we would like to reach in the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process )

glossary.definition: The difference between the initial state and the goal state of the problem-solving process, which indicates the existence of a problem.
glossary.sentence: whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: Executable steps taken to solve a problem, which cause transitions between states in the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The transition from one state to another state takes place by carrying out some action.

glossary.definition: A set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.
glossary.sentence: A state is a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.

glossary.definition: States passed through in a series to get from an initial state to a goal state in the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: This structure indicates that you need to pass through a series of intermediate states to get from an initial state of the problem-solving process to a goal state.

glossary.definition: A graph representing the state space where nodes are located and paths exist between a search start node and a search goal node. Best-First Search allows switching between different paths in this graph.
glossary.sentence: This allows the algorithm to switch between different paths in the state space graph

glossary.definition: Elements in a state space graph that correspond to discrete states of the game; they display states from which only one of the two players can make a move.
glossary.sentence: Its nodes correspond to discrete states of the game...

glossary.definition: The starting node in a state space graph; in the numerical string game example, it contains the initial numerical string and a score of 8 for each player.
glossary.sentence: The root node contains the initial numerical string and a score of 8 for each player...

glossary.definition: Also called a leaf node; a node in a state space graph that corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants

glossary.definition: A terminal node in the game tree reached by the algorithm where a precise heuristic measure is assigned based on the game state (e.g., winner).
glossary.sentence: Step 1: The algorithm goes down using the first unconsidered path on the left until it reaches a leaf node.

glossary.definition: Nodes in a state space graph that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.

glossary.definition: Elements in a state space graph used to represent legal moves in the game.
glossary.sentence: ...and arcs are used to represent legal moves in the game.

glossary.definition: A perspective for defining a state space graph that includes nodes (root, terminal, internal) and arcs representing transitions.
glossary.sentence: We can define the state space graph from two viewpoints: structural and mathematical. The structural viewpoint includes...

glossary.definition: A perspective for defining a state space graph, mentioned alongside the structural viewpoint.
glossary.sentence: We can define the state space graph from two viewpoints: structural and mathematical.

glossary.definition: A game used as an example where the initial state is a blank grid and players make moves by putting marks in empty squares to reach a goal state.
glossary.sentence: Let's take the game of tic-tac-toe as an example.

glossary.definition: States a player wants to reach; in tic-tac-toe, these are states where three marks corresponding to that player are located in a horizontal, vertical or a diagonal row.
glossary.sentence: Each player wants to reach one of the goal states...

glossary.definition: Actions players make to reach a goal state, such as putting marks in empty squares; in a state space graph, these are represented by arcs.
glossary.sentence: To reach a goal state, the players make moves by putting their marks in empty squares.

glossary.definition: A specific state produced by each of the possible moves.
glossary.sentence: Each of the possible moves produces a specific intermediate state.

glossary.definition: Discrete states of the game that change when moves are made.
glossary.sentence: Thus, playing a game involves changing between game states by making legal moves in the game.

glossary.term: deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game where moves are made alternately, node levels are clearly distinguishable, and it follows the principle of a zero-sum game.
glossary.sentence: Since moves are made alternately in a deterministic two-player game of perfect information...

glossary.definition: Levels in a state space graph that are clearly distinguishable in deterministic two-player games of perfect information; each level contains states from which only one of the two players can make a move.
glossary.sentence: ...node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Moves that are allowed in the game, represented by arcs in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Thus, playing a game involves changing between game states by making legal moves in the game.

glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A specific type of game defined at the beginning of the topic, where the same player cannot make two moves rather than only one.
glossary.sentence: ...which would contradict the principle of a zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information as defined at the beginning of this topic.

glossary.definition: A system that can be developed using weak methods (symbolic manipulation or mathematical calculations) or strong methods (incorporating knowledge into the system).
glossary.sentence: Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: Characteristics that characterize the states in the game; they are related to computer implementation, correspond to properties of the game and its course, change values after a move, allow tracking the course of the game, determine a specific state at a specific moment, should be represented in each node, and depend on the game (e.g., score, game board).
glossary.sentence: ...the developer of an intelligent computer system should determine the parameters that characterise the states in the game.

glossary.definition: A critical aspect of a state space graph that should be reduced to avoid overconsumption of computational resources.
glossary.sentence: The complexity of a state space graph is a critical aspect.

glossary.definition: Resources demanded by algorithms; Best-First Search can demand significant computational resources in comparison to other heuristic algorithms, especially if the heuristic evaluation function is not well-defined.
glossary.sentence: However, it can demand significant computational resources in comparison to other heuristic algorithms

glossary.definition: Nodes at the same level that have the same values for all parameters characterizing the states; they are displayed only once per level to save computational resources.
glossary.sentence: Nodes at the same level are identical if they have the same values for all of the parameters that characterise the states of the game.

glossary.definition: A representation associated with a node in the game, such as the string '2-3-3'.
glossary.sentence: a game with the numerical string and a score of 8 for each player (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: Nodes reached when the game stops; in the numerical string example, these are nodes where only one number is left in the numerical string.
glossary.sentence: We can continue in the same way until we reach leaf nodes, in which there is only one number left in the numerical string.

glossary.definition: What a state space graph represents; it organizes and stores data about states and actions of the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The state space graph of the game also represents a data structure.

glossary.definition: An algorithm implemented to allow an intelligent computer system to play the game or solve a problem; it ensures the system chooses only one problem-solving alternative from all alternatives represented in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: To allow an intelligent computer system to play the game, we need to implement a search algorithm.

glossary.definition: One of the possible alternatives chosen by an individual or an intelligent computer system to find a solution to a problem.
glossary.sentence: ...chooses the most appropriate problem-solving alternative from all possible alternatives...

glossary.definition: A topic heading indicating the discussion of state space search.
glossary.sentence: Topic 2.3 - State Space Search

glossary.definition: A system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we are building a recommendation system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse.

glossary.definition: A search type where all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical, because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph and do not have additional information about the quality of the nodes.
glossary.sentence: In the case of uninformed search, all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical, because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph. They do not have additional information about the quality of the nodes.

glossary.definition: Algorithms able to distinguish between paths represented in the state space graph, because heuristic search can take into account such information as the distance between any two settlements, the location of each settlement relative to Paris, the surface of the road leading to each settlement, the number of traffic lights between any two settlements, and many other factors.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms are able to distinguish between paths represented in the state space graph. Therefore, each path differs from all of the others. This is because heuristic search can take into account such information as the distance between any two settlements, the location of each settlement (north, west, etc.) relative to Paris, the surface of the road leading to each settlement, the number of traffic lights between any two settlements, and many other factors.

glossary.definition: Values representing factors such as distance, location, road surface, and traffic lights, showing the quality of each node in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: These factors are represented as numerical values showing the quality of each node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, use the state space graph as a data structure to search for a problem solution, provide a solution path as their output, and have three main operational steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, uninformed search algorithms receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data (Figure 3). They use the state space graph as a data structure to search for a problem solution. The algorithms provide a solution path as their output. They have three main operational steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A data structure that stores a set of elements in sequential order.
glossary.sentence: Search algorithms are often implemented using a data structure called a list. A list stores a set of elements in sequential order.

glossary.definition: A list where Best-First Search stores all of the nodes found so far. The algorithm expands only one node located on the first position in this list. Generated descendants/ancestors are added to this list, and it is ordered based on heuristic measures from best to worst. It is implemented as a priority queue.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this algorithm stores all of the nodes found so far in the OPEN list and expands only one (the best) node located on the first position in the OPEN list.

glossary.definition: A list where nodes are moved after they have been expanded. Initially empty, it contains nodes that have already been processed. Nodes already in the CLOSED list are not considered again by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The CLOSED list is empty because none of the available nodes has been expanded so far.

glossary.definition: A representation consisting of three columns used to represent the OPEN and CLOSED lists in the course: the first column displays the iteration number, the second column displays the OPEN list, and the third column displays the CLOSED list.
glossary.sentence: To represent these lists in our course, we will use a table consisting of three columns (Figure 4). The first column displays the iteration number, the second column displays the OPEN list, and the third column displays the CLOSED list.

glossary.definition: The node taken from the first position of the OPEN list to be expanded or checked. It becomes the current node after being selected from the OPEN list.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: Repetitions avoided by not considering again nodes that are already added to the OPEN or CLOSED lists, and not adding them to the lists.
glossary.sentence: Nodes that are already added to the OPEN or CLOSED lists are not considered again, and they are not added to the lists, with the aim of avoiding cycles and nodes being repeatedly discovered.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm where “depth” means to go deeper and deeper into the state space graph, while expanding nodes; “first” indicates finding the first path that leads to the search goal node and stopping the operation. It starts with the search start node, generates this node’s descendants/ancestors, selects one of the generated nodes as the current node and expands it, then again selects the new current nodes from the recently generated nodes and continues the expansion, going deeper and deeper into the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The idea of Depth-First Search is represented by its title (Figure 5): “depth” means to go deeper and deeper into the state space graph, while expanding nodes; “first” indicates finding the first path that leads to the search goal node and stopping the operation. Therefore, Depth-First Search starts with the search start node. It generates this node’s descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction). The algorithm selects one of the generated nodes as the current node and expands it. Then, it again selects the new current nodes from the recently generated nodes and continues the expansion. In this way, it goes deeper and deeper into the state space graph (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: A type of data structure that the OPEN list becomes in the implementation of Depth-First Search, working according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack. A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A type of search implemented in the state space graph, exemplified by Depth-First Search.
glossary.sentence: Let us consider an example of Depth-First Search by implementing data-driven search in the state space graph that you see in Figure 9.

glossary.definition: The node where the search begins. In the example, node A is the search start node.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is A.

glossary.definition: The node the algorithm aims to find. In the example, node J is the search goal node. The algorithm stops its operation when the current node is the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: The search goal node is J.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that always chooses a node whose heuristic measure is higher (or lower, depending on the function definition) than the heuristic measure of the current node. It is able to find the search goal node very quickly if the heuristic evaluation function is well-defined, but has shortcomings such as the local maximum problem and the plateau problem if the function is not well-defined.
glossary.sentence: if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the highest measure, then Hill Climbing will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes to indicate game states or potential outcomes, which can be precise or intermediate.
glossary.sentence: It assigns a precise heuristic measure of “1” to the leaf node, since this is a state where the maximiser wins the game, because this player has the highest score.

glossary.definition: A function that gives heuristic measures to nodes. If well-defined, Hill Climbing can find the search goal node quickly. If not well-defined, Hill Climbing has shortcomings, and Best-First Search might demand significant computational resources.
glossary.sentence: On the one hand, the Hill Climbing algorithm is able to find the search goal node very quickly if the heuristic evaluation function is well-defined.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction).
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction )

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that does not have the shortcomings of Hill Climbing. In each iteration, it selects the best node found so far, but also keeps all of the nodes that were found before. It stores all found nodes in the OPEN list, expands only the best node located on the first position, and adds generated descendants/ancestors to the OPEN list while ordering the list based on heuristic measures. It is guaranteed to find the search goal node if a path exists, but can demand significant computational resources.
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings.

glossary.definition: A data structure that implements the OPEN list. It works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: Nodes generated from the current node during expansion. The direction (descendants or ancestors) depends on the search direction. They are evaluated by the heuristic evaluation function and added to the OPEN list if not already in OPEN or CLOSED lists.
glossary.sentence: After generating and evaluating direct descendants/ ancestors (depending on the search direction) of the current node, the algorithm adds all of them into the OPEN list

glossary.definition: An algorithm providing a compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search. It selects a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration, expanding all nodes located in the OPEN list simultaneously, evaluating their descendants/ancestors, and choosing the best nodes according to the beam width. Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list.
glossary.sentence: Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: The number of the best nodes examined during search by Beam Search. This number is set before the algorithm starts searching.
glossary.sentence: This number is called a beam width .

glossary.definition: A step in the search process where the algorithm selects a node, checks if it is the goal, expands it (if possible), evaluates generated nodes, updates lists, and prepares for the next step.
glossary.sentence: In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far

glossary.definition: A factor that determines whether generated nodes are referred to as descendants or ancestors.
glossary.sentence: depending on the search direction

glossary.definition: A value assigned to players in the game, such as a score of 8 for each player, which determines the winner (highest score wins).
glossary.sentence: a game with the numerical string and a score of 8 for each player (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A process that traverses the game tree by going down unconsidered paths to leaf nodes, assigning heuristic measures, propagating them up levels, and performing cut-offs based on comparisons.
glossary.sentence: Step 1: The algorithm goes down using the first unconsidered path on the left until it reaches a leaf node.

glossary.definition: A path in the game tree that has not yet been processed by the algorithm, specifically the first unconsidered path on the left or paths below a current node.
glossary.sentence: Step 1: The algorithm goes down using the first unconsidered path on the left until it reaches a leaf node.

glossary.definition: A definitive value assigned to a node, such as 1 (maximiser wins) or -1 (minimiser wins), or a value derived from direct descendants when no other unconsidered descendants exist.
glossary.sentence: It assigns a precise heuristic measure of “1” to the leaf node, since this is a state where the maximiser wins the game, because this player has the highest score.

glossary.definition: A player in the game whose task is to maximise their result, win the game, and achieve the highest score.
glossary.sentence: It assigns a precise heuristic measure of “1” to the leaf node, since this is a state where the maximiser wins the game, because this player has the highest score.

glossary.definition: A position in the hierarchy of the game tree, such as MAX level or MIN level, which determines how heuristic measures are propagated or assigned.
glossary.sentence: When a precise heuristic measure has been assigned, the algorithm goes up one level, along the same path that was used to go down.

glossary.definition: The process by which the algorithm attempts to pass assigned heuristic measures from a node to other nodes along the path used to go down.
glossary.sentence: It then tries to propagate this heuristic measure to other nodes.

glossary.definition: Nodes connected below a current node in the game tree, which may be considered or unconsidered.
glossary.sentence: Step 2: The algorithm assigns a precise heuristic measure that is equal to the heuristic measure of the leaf node, since the node at this level do not have other descendants.

glossary.definition: A non-definitive measure assigned when a precise measure is not possible, indicating a range of potential outcomes (e.g., >=1 or <=-1) based on the node's level and known measures.
glossary.sentence: It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: A level in the game tree associated with the maximiser, where the goal is to maximise the result, and intermediate measures indicate the worst case is the known measure but the best case is higher.
glossary.sentence: It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: A node located above the current node in the game tree path, which may have an assigned heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node also has an intermediate heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A level in the game tree associated with the minimiser, where the goal is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, and intermediate measures indicate the worst case is the known measure but the best case is smaller.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of <=−1, since the node is located at MIN level and the only known heuristic measure is a measure of −1.

glossary.definition: A player in the game whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, prevent the maximiser from winning, and achieve a higher score than the maximiser to win.
glossary.sentence: Step 4 : The algorithm reaches a leaf node and assigns a heuristic measure of −1, since in this state the minimiser has the higher score and wins the game.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node, used in comparison with the ancestor's measure to determine if a cut-off should occur.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the β value) is −1 and the measure of the ancestor (the α value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The heuristic measure of the ancestor of the current node, used in comparison with the current node's β value.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the β value) is −1 and the measure of the ancestor (the α value) is 1.

glossary.definition: An action performed when the β value is smaller than the α value, involving cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: From a comparison of the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: The most similar data object found in the existing dataset, identified based on a similarity metric, whose category/class label is assigned to a new data object.
glossary.sentence: Thus, when searching for an answer to the question of what the category or class of a new data object is, the algorithm finds the most similar neighbour in the existing dataset and assigns its category/class label to the new data object.

glossary.definition: A measure used to determine the similarity of data objects, such as the distance between them, where similar data objects are close to each other.
glossary.sentence: To determine the similarity of data objects, a similarity metric is used such as the distance between data objects because similar data objects are close to each other.

glossary.definition: A concept that makes the basis for the algorithm, sometimes called distance, proximity, or closeness, determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects.
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity (sometimes called distance, proximity, or closeness) makes the basis for the algorithm.

glossary.definition: The similarity of data objects determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects.
glossary.sentence: In this case, the similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity) (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: Feature values for which it is possible to calculate the geometric distance between any pair of data objects, where the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.
glossary.sentence: For continuous feature values, it is possible to calculate the geometric distance between any pair of data objects: the closer to each other the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A distance metric for continuous feature values that measures the square root of the sum of the squares of the differences between the value of the i-th feature of a new data object and the value of the i-th feature of an existing data object in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: The Euclidean distance measures the square root of the sum of the squares of the differences between the value of the i- th feature of a new data object and the value of the i- th feature of an existing data object in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A distance metric for continuous feature values that calculates the sum of absolute differences between feature values.
glossary.sentence: The Manhattan distance calculates the sum of absolute differences between feature values.

glossary.definition: A representation of a data object's features used in distance calculations, such as calculating the distance between the feature vector of a new data object and the feature vector of an existing data object.
glossary.sentence: Both metrics calculate the distance between the feature vector of a new data object and the feature vector of an existing data object in the dataset.

glossary.definition: Feature values represented as discrete categories/classes.
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A distance metric used for categorical feature values that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ; if the values of the same feature are the same, the distance is 0, otherwise it is 1.
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: Datasets that include both continuous and categorical data.
glossary.sentence: As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features (Kubat, 2017):

glossary.definition: Requirements that any distance metric must satisfy: 1. the distance must never be negative; 2. the distance between two identical feature vectors is zero; 3. the distance from x to y is the same as the distance from y to x; 4. the metric must satisfy the triangular inequality.
glossary.sentence: However, any distance metric must satisfy the following requirements (Kubat, 2017): 1. the distance must never be negative; 2. the distance between two identical feature vectors is zero; 3. the distance from x to y is the same as the distance from y to x ; 4. the metric must satisfy the triangular inequality:

glossary.definition: One nearest neighbour version of the algorithm where the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closest data object in the dataset and taking the same class that the closest data object has.
glossary.sentence: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A version of the algorithm that uses a hyperparameter k to define the number of nearest data objects considered to identify the output value for a new data object, applying a voting mechanism to select the class with the majority vote.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: A value set by the developer before the operation of the algorithm that defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: A process where, after finding distances and sorting data objects in ascending order, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and takes the plurality vote of the neighbours (majority vote in binary classification) to assign a label to a new data object.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A classification problem where there are only two classes, requiring k to be an odd number to prevent ties.
glossary.sentence: If the problem is binary classification (there are only two classes), then k should be an odd number to prevent ties (Kubat, 2017; Russell & Norvig, 2010);

glossary.definition: A classification problem where using an odd number of nearest neighbours does not prevent ties, requiring a mechanism to choose between different classes if the number of data objects belonging to each is equal.
glossary.sentence: In multiclass classification, using an odd number of nearest neighbours does not prevent ties.

glossary.definition: A measure used to select the value of k, where it is practical to create a plot between error rate and k values to choose the k value providing a minimum error rate.
glossary.sentence: It is practical to create a plot between error rate and k values, later choosing the k value providing a minimum error rate.

glossary.definition: A process in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering, starting with each data object in a single cluster and merging until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level.
glossary.sentence: Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: A top-down clustering approach that starts with a cluster containing all data objects and recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters at each hierarchy level, aiming to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity, until each data object makes a cluster.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach.

glossary.definition: Methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods.

glossary.definition: A linkage method where, in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters' most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated

glossary.definition: A linkage method where, in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated

glossary.definition: A linkage method where the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster

glossary.definition: An output of hierarchical clustering; a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset, providing a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format and insight into the way how clusters were formed.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering.

glossary.definition: Components of a dendrogram that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height, which serves as an indicator of cluster similarity.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height

glossary.definition: Terminal points of clades in a dendrogram corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.
glossary.sentence: leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: A line usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm; the number of vertical lines intersected by this line represents the number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: An algorithm where the entire cluster hierarchy represents an ordered sequence of cluster merges, which does not demand to specify the number of clusters before the algorithm operation, but requires deciding where to cut the dendrogram and which linkage method to use.
glossary.sentence: Summarising the information about the hierarchical clustering given in this topic, it is worth mentioning that the entire cluster hierarchy represents an ordered sequence of cluster merges

glossary.definition: Metrics used to compare the performance of different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters; they depend on the task (classification, regression, or clustering).
glossary.sentence: Metrics for evaluating the machine learning model performance are used to: compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.

glossary.definition: A supervised machine learning task that demands making predictions in a continuous range, resulting in a real value output, where actual labels are given in the dataset to determine the correctness of the predicted labels.
glossary.sentence: In general, the regression task demands making predictions in a continuous range.

glossary.definition: A metric that measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects, providing positive non-zero values.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning task that artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out.
glossary.sentence: Unsupervised Machine Learning

glossary.definition: A task of supervised machine learning for which artificial neural networks can be used.
glossary.sentence: Classification Task

glossary.definition: Cells that make up the human brain. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus, many dendrites, and a single axon. The primary activity of a neuron is to fire an electrical impulse along its axon, which depends upon the activity at the synapses. Human mental activity consists primarily of electrochemical activity in networks of neurons.
glossary.sentence: Biological Neuron

glossary.definition: The cell body of a biological neuron, containing a nucleus.
glossary.sentence: Soma

glossary.definition: Root-like extensions from the cell body of a biological neuron that look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Dendrites

glossary.definition: A single root-like extension from the cell body of a biological neuron that eventually splits into branches, each terminating in an endbulb. A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse.
glossary.sentence: Axon

glossary.definition: The site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron. When a signal from a sending neuron reaches the synapse, it generates an electrical signal in the dendrites of the receiving neuron. Learning in human neural networks is associated with synapses, which can be strengthened by carrying more electrical current, leading to higher conductivity and weight.
glossary.sentence: Synapse

glossary.definition: A type of synapse where signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing. It is thought of as having a positive weight.
glossary.sentence: Excitatory Synapse

glossary.definition: A type of synapse where signals reaching the neuron's dendrites inhibit firing. It is thought of as having a negative weight.
glossary.sentence: Inhibitory Synapse

glossary.definition: A value specific to a neuron; if the total input to the neuron exceeds this threshold, the neuron will fire.
glossary.sentence: Threshold

glossary.definition: A highly complex, non-linear and parallel processing system that processes information simultaneously throughout the whole network rather than at a specific location. It consists of cells called neurons.
glossary.sentence: Human Brain

glossary.definition: A mathematical model of the neuron defined by McCulloch and Pitts in 1943, which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks. It has one or more inputs, a set of real-valued weights, an output, a function for combining input signals, a transfer (activation) function, and a bias input. It operates by receiving input signals, combining them using a specific function, passing the value through an activation function to determine the output signal, and sending the output signal to the environment or other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Artificial Neuron

glossary.definition: One or more input signals of an artificial neuron, represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons. They typically correspond to the values of data object features in the dataset and are labelled by indexed x.
glossary.sentence: Inputs

glossary.definition: A set of real-valued values (one for each input) in an artificial neuron that express the strength or importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output. They are the basic means of long-term memory in artificial neural networks, determined in the process of neural network learning, and typically represented by indexed w.
glossary.sentence: Weights

glossary.definition: A numerical value produced by an artificial neuron that can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons. It is usually represented by indexed y.
glossary.sentence: Output

glossary.definition: A function for combining input signals in an artificial neuron that sums up the weighted values of inputs, providing a single numerical value. It is typically used to combine input signals, though other functions are allowed.
glossary.sentence: Summation Function

glossary.definition: A function in an artificial neuron that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships. Common choices include the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.
glossary.sentence: Transfer (Activation) Function

glossary.definition: An input commonly applied in an artificial neuron that provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin. It is typically indexed with 0 index and commonly set to one, with its weight tuned by the learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Bias Input

glossary.definition: One of the four common choices of activation functions.
glossary.sentence: Step Function

glossary.definition: One of the four common choices of activation functions.
glossary.sentence: Sign Function

glossary.definition: One of the four common choices of activation functions.
glossary.sentence: Linear Function

glossary.definition: One of the four common choices of activation functions.
glossary.sentence: Sigmoid Function

glossary.definition: A concept closely related to artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Deep Learning

glossary.definition: Researchers who defined a mathematical model of the neuron in 1943, laying fundamentals for artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: McCulloch and Pitts

glossary.definition: A repository where knowledge is added, described, represented, and stored, often used in intelligent computer systems and expert systems.
glossary.sentence: Thus not all intelligent systems need the knowledge base because intelligent computer systems can also be developed using weak methods (symbolic manipulation or mathematical calculations).

glossary.definition: Examples of systems developed using weak methods.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning applications and search-based planning systems are examples of such systems.

glossary.definition: Examples of systems developed using weak methods.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning applications and search-based planning systems are examples of such systems.

glossary.definition: Anyone who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain, and who has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base, but is not a specialist in all problem domains, working in close collaboration with the domain expert.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: An automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base, offering a variety of user interfaces (e.g., creation of diagrams or structured descriptions) and including tools for analysis, generation, and consistency checking.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A problem type addressed by expert systems involving forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.
glossary.sentence: Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: A problem type addressed by expert systems involving projecting probable consequences of given situations.
glossary.sentence: Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations.

glossary.definition: A problem type addressed by expert systems involving determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.
glossary.sentence: Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: A problem type addressed by expert systems involving finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: A problem type addressed by expert systems involving devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: A problem type addressed by expert systems involving comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: A problem type addressed by expert systems involving governing the behaviour of a complex environment.
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: Means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge, creating a knowledge representation or description.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: Schemas that represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
