glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge

glossary.definition: Schemas that represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them, based on associationist theories of human mind.
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them

glossary.definition: Schemas that use a graph to represent knowledge. Each node represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts. Relations are not represented explicitly by arcs but use a referencing principle where one node references another node by its name.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge.

glossary.term: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: Schemas that represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: Theories of human mind developed in psychology that postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation method that uses several kinds of relations to represent knowledge, allowing ease of representation and understanding, defining a variety of relations between objects/concepts, representing knowledge in a structured way, supporting inheritance, and having a simple inference engine. However, they represent only declarative knowledge, lack well-defined formal semantics, can introduce ambiguity, and do not allow representing specific types of knowledge like negation or disjunction.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is a feature of semantic networks (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A connection used in semantic networks to relate concepts, objects, parts, properties, or values. Directions of relations are specified (e.g., from narrower to broader, from parts to whole, from object to property). Types include subclass-class, instance-class/set/type/kind, part-whole, object-property, property-value, and linguistic relations.
glossary.sentence: Several kinds of relations are used in semantic networks:

glossary.definition: A relation represented by titles such as “is a”, “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of”, or “is a kind of”. It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts. The direction is from a narrower concept to a broader concept.
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a”...

glossary.definition: A category that includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title “is an instance/example of”. It allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to. The direction is from an example to a class/set/type/kind.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title “Part of”. It allows relating parts of an object to the whole object. Without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance. The direction is from parts to whole objects.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8).

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties. The direction is from the object to a property.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”.

glossary.definition: A characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values. This relation can come only after the relation “property/has a property of”. The direction is from the property to its value.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”.

glossary.definition: Relations that allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations...

glossary.definition: A rule to be followed when relating objects/concepts by defined relationship types: reading any two objects/concepts in the direction of the arc of the relationship should yield a meaningful fact.
glossary.sentence: When relating objects/concepts by the types of relationships defined previosuly , the golden rule should be followed...

glossary.definition: A feature of semantic networks. It is a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network. Specifically, a subclass/subset/subtype/subkind node inherits knowledge from a class/set/type/kind node, or an instance inherits knowledge from a subclass/subset/subtype/subkind node or a class/set/type/kind node. It allows reducing the size of the knowledge base and is performed dynamically by the intelligent system when needed.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is a feature of semantic networks (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A component needed to provide possibilities for an intelligent system to use knowledge represented in the form of a semantic network. It is based on principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, explore relations of the object/concept to other objects/concept by following direction of arcs; to find knowledge about a specific object/concept, check local relations; if knowledge is not found, perform inheritance if possible.
glossary.sentence: To provide possibilities for an intelligent system to use knowledge represented in the form of a semantic network, the inference engine is needed.

glossary.definition: Knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts, which is the only type of knowledge represented by semantic networks.
glossary.sentence: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts );

glossary.definition: An example of structured knowledge representation schemas developed by Marvin Minsky in 1975. Based on the idea that when encountering a new situation, one selects from memory a structure called a Frame, which is a remembered framework adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary. It implies an individual has structured knowledge of a past situation in memory and supplements or changes it in new situations.
glossary.sentence: Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas.

glossary.definition: A remembered framework selected from memory to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary when encountering a new situation or making a substantial change in one's view of the present problem.
glossary.sentence: This is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.” (Minsky, 1975)
